name: Linear API
what_it_does: Linear's GraphQL API for programmatic access to issues, projects, cycles, teams, and workflows. Popular with AI agent builders for its clean schema, comprehensive webhooks, and developer-first design. Agents use it to create issues, update status, query sprint state, and automate engineering workflows.
use_cases: Automatically creating issues from agent-detected bugs or alerts
Querying open issues and cycle state for sprint planning agents
Updating issue status and assignees as agent workflows complete tasks
Building automated triage agents that label and prioritize incoming issues
Syncing Linear issues with external systems (GitHub PRs, Jira, Slack)
Generating sprint reports and velocity metrics from issue data
not_for: Teams not using Linear for issue tracking
Agents needing REST API — Linear is GraphQL-only
Large enterprise teams needing Jira-level customization
best_when: An agent needs to manage engineering workflows for a software team that uses Linear — creating issues, tracking sprint progress, or automating triage.
avoid_when: Your team uses Jira or GitHub Issues, or you prefer a REST API over GraphQL.

auth.notes: Personal API keys for direct agent use (available in Linear settings under API). OAuth 2.0 for user-context integrations. API keys have team-scoped access. OAuth supports fine-grained scopes (read:issues, write:issues, etc.). Rate limits apply per API key. Recommended to create dedicated API keys per agent.

pricing.free_tier_limits: Free for up to 250 issues; API access included in all tiers
pricing.paid_tiers: Basic $8/user/month, Business $14/user/month; API access unchanged across tiers
pricing.notes: API access is available on all plans including free. Rate limits may differ between tiers. Linear is reasonably priced for small to mid-size engineering teams.

performance.rate_limits: 1,500 requests/hour per API key; complex GraphQL queries may count more. Webhook delivery has retry logic.

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: GraphQL errors follow the standard errors array format with message, locations, and extensions containing a userPresentableMessage. Error codes are descriptive. Rate limit errors return HTTP 429 with a Retry-After header. Authentication errors are clear.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: Issue creation is not idempotent — duplicate issues can be created. Best practice: search for existing issues before creating. Updates by ID are idempotent. Webhooks have idempotency keys for deduplication.
known_agent_gotchas: GraphQL-only API means agents must construct queries manually or use the official SDK — no simple REST endpoint
Issue IDs are UUIDs internally but displayed as team-prefixed identifiers (ENG-123) — always use UUID for API calls
Cursor-based pagination requires careful handling — Linear cursors are opaque base64 strings
Mutations that create issues don't prevent duplicates — agents must implement their own deduplication
Team ID is required for issue creation — agents must look up team IDs, they are not predictable
Webhook payloads are typed by resourceType but schema varies — agents need per-type handling
Archive != delete — archived issues still count against some limits and appear in API responses by default
